> ## Documentation Index
> Fetch the complete documentation index at: https://code.claude.com/docs/llms.txt
> Use this file to discover all available pages before exploring further.

# Run agents in parallel

> Compare the ways Claude Code can take on multiple tasks at once: subagents, agent view, agent teams, and dynamic workflows.

[Subagents](/en/sub-agents), [agent view](/en/agent-view), [agent teams](/en/agent-teams), and [dynamic workflows](/en/workflows) each parallelize work in a different way. The right one depends on whether you want to stay in each conversation yourself, hand tasks off and check back later, or have Claude coordinate a group of workers for you.

| Approach                           | What it gives you                                                                                                                                         | Use it when                                                                                                                                                                                         |
| :--------------------------------- | :-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | :-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| [Subagents](/en/sub-agents)        | Delegated workers inside one session that do a side task in their own context and return a summary                                                        | A side task would flood your main conversation with search results, logs, or file contents you won't reference again                                                                                |
| [Agent view](/en/agent-view)       | One screen to dispatch and monitor sessions running in the background, opened with `claude agents`. Research preview                                      | You have several independent tasks and want to hand them off, check status at a glance, and step in only when one needs you                                                                         |
| [Agent teams](/en/agent-teams)     | Multiple coordinated sessions with a shared task list and inter-agent messaging, managed by a lead. Experimental and disabled by default                  | You want Claude to split a project into pieces, assign them, and keep the workers in sync                                                                                                           |
| [Dynamic workflows](/en/workflows) | A script that runs many subagents and cross-checks their results, for work too big to coordinate one turn at a time or that needs more than a single pass | A job outgrows a handful of subagents, or you want findings verified against each other: a codebase-wide audit, a 500-file migration, cross-checked research, or a plan drafted from several angles |

In every approach the workers are Claude sessions. To involve a different tool, expose it to Claude as an [MCP server](/en/mcp).

Two more tools support this work without being a way to run agents themselves:

* [Worktrees](/en/worktrees) give each session a separate git checkout, so parallel sessions never edit the same files. Use them for sessions you run yourself. Agent view moves each dispatched session into its own worktree automatically, and subagents you spawn can each get one too.
* [`/batch`](/en/commands) is a [skill](/en/skills) that has Claude split one large change into 5 to 30 worktree-isolated subagents that each open a pull request. It's a packaged use of subagents and worktrees, not a separate coordination style.

A few other features run Claude without you driving each step, but they solve a different problem than splitting work across agents:

* A [background bash command](/en/interactive-mode#background-bash-commands) runs one shell command without blocking the conversation. It doesn't spawn an agent.
* A [forked subagent](/en/sub-agents#fork-the-current-conversation) is a subagent that inherits your full conversation context instead of starting fresh. It's a way to spawn a subagent, not a separate surface.
* A [routine](/en/routines) runs a session on a schedule in Anthropic's cloud, not in parallel on your machine.

<Note>
  Running several sessions or subagents at once multiplies token usage. See [Costs](/en/costs) for usage and rate-limit details.
</Note>

## Choose an approach

The right approach depends on who coordinates the work, whether the workers need to communicate, and whether they edit the same files:

* **Who coordinates the work?**
  * Claude delegates and collects results inside one conversation: [subagents](/en/sub-agents)
  * You hand off independent tasks and check back later: [agent view](/en/agent-view)
  * Claude plans, assigns, and supervises a group of workers: [agent teams](/en/agent-teams), experimental and disabled by default
  * A script holds the plan instead of Claude's turn-by-turn judgment: [dynamic workflows](/en/workflows). See [how workflows compare to subagents and skills](/en/workflows#when-to-use-a-workflow)
* **Do the workers need to talk to each other?** Subagents report results back to the conversation that spawned them, and agent view sessions report only to you. Teammates in an agent team share a task list and message each other directly.
* **Do the tasks touch the same files?** Isolate the work with [worktrees](/en/worktrees). Subagents and sessions you run yourself can each use a separate worktree. Agent teams don't isolate teammates in worktrees, so [partition the work](/en/agent-teams#avoid-file-conflicts) so each teammate owns a different set of files.

## Check on running work

The command for checking on running work depends on which approach you used:

* For background sessions, `claude agents` opens [agent view](/en/agent-view): one screen showing every session, its state, and which ones need your input.
* For subagents in the current session, named background subagents appear in the @-mention typeahead with their status. {/* min-version: 2.1.198 */}As of v2.1.198, `/agents` no longer opens a panel; it prints a notice pointing to the subagent file locations. To [create and edit custom subagents](/en/sub-agents#configure-subagents), ask Claude or edit the files directly. Despite the similar name, `/agents` is separate from `claude agents`.
* For anything running in the background of the current session, `/tasks` lists each item and lets you check on, attach to, or stop it.
* For dynamic workflows, `/workflows` lists running and completed runs, the phase each is in, and how many agents have finished.

For a desktop view of all your sessions, see [parallel sessions in the desktop app](/en/desktop#work-in-parallel-with-sessions).

## Learn more

Each guide below covers setup and configuration for one approach:

* [Create custom subagents](/en/sub-agents): define reusable specialists and control which tools they can use.
* [Manage agents with agent view](/en/agent-view): dispatch sessions, watch their state, and attach when one needs you.
* [Orchestrate agent teams](/en/agent-teams): set up a lead and teammates, assign tasks, and review their work.
* [Orchestrate dynamic workflows](/en/workflows): run a bundled workflow or have Claude write one that runs many subagents and verifies their findings against each other.
* [Run parallel sessions with worktrees](/en/worktrees): start Claude in an isolated checkout, control what gets copied in, and clean up afterward.

Two more tools support this work without being a way to run agents themselves:

* [Worktrees](/en/worktrees) give each session a separate git checkout, so parallel sessions never edit the same files. Use them for sessions you run yourself. Agent view moves each dispatched session into its own worktree automatically, and subagents you spawn can each get one too.
* [`/batch`](/en/commands) is a [skill](/en/skills) that has Claude split one large change into 5 to 30 worktree-isolated subagents that each open a pull request. It's a packaged use of subagents and worktrees, not a separate coordination style.

A few other features run Claude without you driving each step, but they solve a different problem than splitting work across agents:

* A [background bash command](/en/interactive-mode#background-bash-commands) runs one shell command without blocking the conversation. It doesn't spawn an agent.
* A [forked subagent](/en/sub-agents#fork-the-current-conversation) is a subagent that inherits your full conversation context instead of starting fresh. It's a way to spawn a subagent, not a separate surface.
* A [routine](/en/routines) runs a session on a schedule in Anthropic's cloud, not in parallel on your machine.

<Note>
  Running several sessions or subagents at once multiplies token usage. See [Costs](/en/costs) for usage and rate-limit details.
</Note>

## Choose an approach

The right approach depends on who coordinates the work, whether the workers need to communicate, and whether they edit the same files:

* **Who coordinates the work?**
  * Claude delegates and collects results inside one conversation: [subagents](/en/sub-agents)
  * You hand off independent tasks and check back later: [agent view](/en/agent-view)
  * Claude plans, assigns, and supervises a group of workers: [agent teams](/en/agent-teams), experimental and disabled by default
  * A script holds the plan instead of Claude's turn-by-turn judgment: [dynamic workflows](/en/workflows). See [how workflows compare to subagents and skills](/en/workflows#when-to-use-a-workflow)
* **Do the workers need to talk to each other?** Subagents report results back to the conversation that spawned them, and agent view sessions report only to you. Teammates in an agent team share a task list and message each other directly.
* **Do the tasks touch the same files?** Isolate the work with [worktrees](/en/worktrees). Subagents and sessions you run yourself can each use a separate worktree. Agent teams don't isolate teammates in worktrees, so [partition the work](/en/agent-teams#avoid-file-conflicts) so each teammate owns a different set of files.

## Check on running work

The command for checking on running work depends on which approach you used:

* For background sessions, `claude agents` opens [agent view](/en/agent-view): one screen showing every session, its state, and which ones need your input.
* For subagents in the current session, named background subagents appear in the @-mention typeahead with their status. {/* min-version: 2.1.198 */}As of v2.1.198, `/agents` no longer opens a panel; it prints a notice pointing to the subagent file locations. To [create and edit custom subagents](/en/sub-agents#configure-subagents), ask Claude or edit the files directly. Despite the similar name, `/agents` is separate from `claude agents`.
* For anything running in the background of the current session, `/tasks` lists each item and lets you check on, attach to, or stop it.
* For dynamic workflows, `/workflows` lists running and completed runs, the phase each is in, and how many agents have finished.

For a desktop view of all your sessions, see [parallel sessions in the desktop app](/en/desktop#work-in-parallel-with-sessions).

## Learn more

Each guide below covers setup and configuration for one approach:

* [Create custom subagents](/en/sub-agents): define reusable specialists and control which tools they can use.
* [Manage agents with agent view](/en/agent-view): dispatch sessions, watch their state, and attach when one needs you.
* [Orchestrate agent teams](/en/agent-teams): set up a lead and teammates, assign tasks, and review their work.
* [Orchestrate dynamic workflows](/en/workflows): run a bundled workflow or have Claude write one that runs many subagents and verifies their findings against each other.
* [Run parallel sessions with worktrees](/en/worktrees): start Claude in an isolated checkout, control what gets copied in, and clean up afterward.

For the model field, I can use short aliases like sonnet or opus, full model IDs, or inherit as the default — these same values work with the --model flag. The sonnet alias currently resolves to Claude Sonnet 5 on the Anthropic API (requires Claude Code v2.1.197+), though it maps to different versions depending on the platform being used, and in the user's specific environment their custom settings have pinned all aliases to Sonnet 5.

There's a five-level precedence hierarchy for scope that determines which configuration takes priority.

The /agents command behavior changed in v2.1.198 — it now just prints file locations rather than opening an interactive wizard like it did in earlier versions, and I should note that the shell command claude agents (without a slash) is completely separate and opens Agent View instead.

When a subagent is invoked, the main agent can pass a model parameter for that specific call, which follows its own resolution order.  The CLAUDE_CODE_SUBAGENT_MODEL environment variable takes highest priority and forces a model onto all subagents, overriding both per-invocation parameters and the agent's frontmatter definition. If that's not set, the per-call override wins over the agent definition, which wins over the main conversation's model. I should also mention that subagents inherit the main conversation's extended-thinking state with no per-subagent toggle, and the org's availableModels allowlist applies to subagent models too — disallowed values silently fall back to the inherited default.

To set a default model across all subagents, I'd use CLAUDE_CODE_SUBAGENT_MODEL as an environment variable in settings.json (under the `env` section) or as a shell env var — there's no plain settings.json field for this. Now I'm connecting this back to what the user actually wants to achieve with their existing setup.

They want the main conversation on Fable 5 (already set via ANTHROPIC_MODEL) and subagents on Sonnet 5. Since CLAUDE_CODE_SUBAGENT_MODEL isn't currently set in their settings.json, subagents will fall through the resolution chain and end up inheriting Fable 5 from the main conversation unless they explicitly set a model in their frontmatter or the parent Claude passes a per-invocation override. To guarantee Sonnet 5 for all subagents, they can either add CLAUDE_CODE_SUBAGENT_MODEL to the env block in settings.json (which applies globally and can't be overridden per-agent), or add a model field to the frontmatter of individual custom subagent files if they only want it for specific agents. content.text:
## Summary: Subagent model configuration in Claude Code (verified against current docs, last updated 2026-07-06)

Sources: [sub-agents](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents.md), [model-config](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/model-config.md), [agents](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agents.md), [agent-view](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-view.md)

### 1. `.claude/agents/*.md` frontmatter schema

Only `name` and `description` are required. Full field list:

| Field | Accepts |
|---|---|
| `name` | lowercase letters/hyphens, unique within scope; the value hooks see as `agent_type` |
| `description` | text — when Claude should delegate to this subagent |
| `tools` | allowlist (e.g. `Read, Grep, Glob`); supports `Agent(type1, type2)` to restrict which subagents it can spawn |
| `disallowedTools` | denylist, applied before `tools` |
| `model` | **`sonnet`, `opus`, `haiku`, `fable`** (aliases) · a **full model ID** like `claude-sonnet-5` or `claude-opus-4-8` (same values `--model` accepts) · **`inherit`**. Omitted = defaults to `inherit` |
| `permissionMode` | `default`, `acceptEdits`, `auto`, `dontAsk`, `bypassPermissions`, `plan`, or `manual` (alias for `default`, v2.1.200+) |
| `maxTurns`, `skills`, `mcpServers`, `hooks`, `memory` (`user`/`project`/`local`), `background`, `effort`, `isolation` (`worktree`), `color`, `initialPrompt` | see docs for details |

**Does `model: sonnet` resolve to Sonnet 5?** Yes, currently, on the Anthropic API: *"opus resolves to Opus 4.8 and sonnet resolves to Sonnet 5"* (requires Claude Code **v2.1.197+**). On Bedrock/Vertex/Foundry it's still Sonnet 4.5; on Claude Platform on AWS, Sonnet 4.6. **This is exactly the kind of thing that's version- and provider-dependent** — aliases "update over time," so pin a full ID if you need certainty.

**Scope precedence** (name collision → higher wins):
1. Managed/org settings `.claude/agents/` (highest)
2. `--agents` CLI flag (session-only, not persisted)
3. `.claude/agents/` — **project**, discovered walking up from cwd to repo root
4. `~/.claude/agents/` — **user**, all projects
5. Plugin's `agents/` directory (lowest)

Your project currently has no `.claude/agents/*.md` files, so this would be a first-time setup here.

### 2. `/agents` command — version-dependent, and easy to confuse with `claude agents`

- **As of v2.1.198** (current): `/agents` **no longer opens a wizard**. It just prints a notice pointing to the `.claude/agents/` / `~/.claude/agents/` file locations — creation/editing is done by asking Claude or hand-editing the files.
- **v2.1.197 and earlier**: `/agents` opened an interactive terminal wizard with a **Running** tab (live subagents) and a **Library** tab (create/edit/delete).
- **Don't confuse it with `claude agents`** (no slash, run from the shell) — that opens *Agent View*, an unrelated feature for dispatching/monitoring background sessions, not subagent definitions.

Since I can't run `claude --version` myself, confirm your version if this matters.

### 3. Per-call model override vs. frontmatter — override wins

When Claude invokes a subagent via the Agent/Task tool, it can pass a `model` parameter for that specific call. Full resolution order:

1. **`CLAUDE_CODE_SUBAGENT_MODEL`** env var (if set to anything but `inherit`) — wins over everything
2. **Per-invocation `model` parameter**
3. **Subagent definition's `model` frontmatter**
4. **Main conversation's model**

So: **the per-call override beats the frontmatter's `model`**, but an org's `availableModels` allowlist is checked at every level and silently falls back to the inherited model if a value is excluded. Also as of v2.1.198, subagents inherit the main conversation's extended-thinking on/off state — there's no separate per-subagent thinking toggle.

### 4. Yes — `CLAUDE_CODE_SUBAGENT_MODEL` forces one model onto every subagent

> *"The model to use for all subagents and agent teams. Overrides the per-invocation `model` parameter and the subagent definition's `model` frontmatter. Set to `inherit` to use normal model resolution instead."*

There's no plain top-level settings.json key for this (like `"subagentModel"`) — it's exposed only as an environment variable, so you'd set it inside the `env` block, same as your other `ANTHROPIC_*` vars.

---

### Applied to your settings.json

You already have `ANTHROPIC_MODEL: "claude-fable-5"` (main conversation = Fable 5) and `ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_SONNET_MODEL: "claude-sonnet-5"` (also `OPUS`/`HAIKU` pinned to the same ID). Two things worth flagging:

- **`CLAUDE_CODE_SUBAGENT_MODEL` is not set today**, so any subagent that omits `model:` in its frontmatter (or is a built-in like Explore/Plan/general-purpose, which default to `inherit`) will run on **Fable 5**, not Sonnet 5 — inherit means "same as main conversation," not "Sonnet."
- To get Sonnet 5 on subagents while keeping Fable 5 on the main thread, you have two options:
  - **Global, unoverridable**: add `"CLAUDE_CODE_SUBAGENT_MODEL": "claude-sonnet-5"` to your `env` block — forces *every* subagent onto Sonnet 5, ignoring any per-agent `model:` frontmatter.
  - **Per-agent**: put `model: claude-sonnet-5` (or `model: sonnet`, since your `ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_SONNET_MODEL` is already pinned there) in each `.claude/agents/*.md` file's frontmatter, leaving other subagents free to inherit Fable 5.

One more caveat: your `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL` (`https://nexaxis.ai`) is a non-first-party/custom gateway, not `api.anthropic.com`. Docs note that on a custom base URL, Claude Code **skips client-side validation of model-ID strings** and passes them straight through — the provider-specific alias defaults quoted above (e.g., "sonnet → Sonnet 5") formally apply to the Anthropic API, Bedrock, Vertex, Foundry, and Claude Platform on AWS specifically. Since you've explicitly pinned every alias via `ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_*_MODEL`, you've already followed the docs' recommended practice for gateway deployments ("pin model versions... before rolling out"), so this ambiguity shouldn't bite you — just know that any resolution error would surface from nexaxis.ai's response rather than a Claude Code preflight check. This also matches why `ENABLE_TOOL_SEARCH: "true"` is present in your config: it's required to keep MCP tool search working, since it's disabled by default on any non-first-party `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL`.

If you want me to actually draft the `.claude/agents/*.md` file(s), or wire up `CLAUDE_CODE_SUBAGENT_MODEL` in settings.json, I (or `/update-config`) can do that directly — just say which approach you want.
